dialogos.texto_en: (I wonder if that's...Yotsuba and Raiha\n over there at that souvenir shop.)
It's tough deciding on souvenirs.\nMy friend said they want something delicious,\nbut there are too many options to choose from.
With chinsuko you're on the safe side.\nAnother classic might be sata andagi?
Yeah, yeah, if it's a classic,\nit means it's delicious, right?
But don't you also want to surprise them\nwith something more adventurous?\nThat sounds like fun.
Oh, you. If anything, it sounds risky.
Ah, Nino.\nUesugi-san, too.
You're looking for souvenirs\nfor your friends.
Yeah. I'm torn between going for something a little\nadventurous, or sticking with a classic.
This one looks like it could have a strong impact.\nMimiga jerky!
Mimiga are pig ears, right?\nThey even have jerky of that, huh.
Isn't that a bit too daring?\nAnd it doesn't seem suitable for sharing.
That's right, sharing is important too!\nWe need something that everyone can share.
And, looking closely, there are a lot of souvenirs here\nthat are snacks made of pork.
Wow, you're right.\nI know about rafute and all, but%p-2;――
I wonder what this 'nakami-jiru' is.
It's a mystery to me.\nI have no idea.
Is it the taste between two opposite flavors?\nLike the middle of spicy and sweet?
So what flavor is that, then?
I wonder?\nIt's not spicy or sweet...\nIt's turning into a riddle.
Ah!\nCould the 'naka' here mean organs?
Ok, so there's a double meaning,\nboth 'taste' and 'contents.'
Yeah, that's what I was thinking.
So, it could be a soup made with pig organs?\nThat's a possibility.
Right, time to check the answer!
Oh yeah, we can look up the answer on our smartphones.
Wonder if it's like pork miso soup.
I see. In Okinawa, they call pork soup 'nakami-jiru,'\ndon't they.
Then, they should have just said it was pork soup\nin the first place.
Oh, I see what you mean.\nIf we bought it and opened it up to find pork miso soup,\nwe would have been disappointed.
If it's not pork soup,\nthen it's probably another Okinawan dish.
It might be something other than pork miso soup,\nsomething a little different...
...That's right.\n'Naka' here is referring to contents,\nwhich means organs.
I get it, it's a soup using organs, huh.
Right, time to check the answer!
Oh yeah, we can look up the answer on our smartphones.
I think the kanji for 'mi' is 'body,' not 'taste.'\nIt means a soup containing pig organs.
Ah, you're right!\nOf course you'd know that, Uesugi-san.
If it's a soup made of organs,\nit could be an offal stew.
I see! It's easier to imagine it that way.
Right, time to check the answer!
Oh yeah, we can look up the answer on our smartphones.
Umm...
I think it's a dish made from pig organs after all. It\nseems to be a soup that is simmered in bonito broth,\nto enjoy the texture and chewiness of the offal.
I get it.
Apparently, there's also a dish that uses pig blood\ncalled 'chiricha.' Seems pretty unusual.
BLOOD is edible?!
There's an interesting saying in Okinawa:\nthey eat everything from a pig\nexcept its squeal, and it seems to be true.
Chiricha is pretty delicious, they say!\nIf I have the chance, I'd like to try it.
I'm curious about the taste.\nI can't imagine...
However, nakami-jiru wouldn't be suitable as a souvenir.\nIt'd be difficult to share with others.
Mmmhh, maybe sticking to the classics\nis better than taking risks.
Well, I've never been in a situation where\nI've received something inedible as a souvenir.
That might be true for you, big bro.
Oh, I have an idea.\nHow about buying some classic items as well as local sweets,\nand wrap them up separately?
Now, you'd be able to enjoy various sweets\nand it could also be a great conversation topic.
Ohh! Good idea!
In that case, we could also pay special attention\nto the wrapping paper! Should we look for paper bags\nwith Okinawan patterns?
It doesn't have to be a special bag.\nJust decorating a regular bag with Okinawan-style\nwashi tape could make a difference.
Oh, that's true. This way, it's possible\nto make it look very 'Okinawa-ish.'\nYou would think of that, Nino-san.
(...Hm?\n Hey, now that I'm thinking about it%p-2;――%p;)
(I'm not really leading Nino, am I?)
(Damn! What am I doing?!\n I got so caught up in the conversation\n that I completely forgot my intention...!)
